questions: Using the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics' Occupational Outlook Handbook projections for the 2024–2034 period, identify two distinct computer-related occupations that share a projected job outlook growth rate of exactly 15% ('Much faster than average'). These two occupations must be distinguished by their required work experience in a related occupation: one lists 'None' while the other lists '5 years or more'. For each of these two identified occupations, provide the 2024 median annual pay, the total projected numeric employment change over the decade, and the estimated average number of annual openings.

Identify the specific combined occupational group monitored by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics that reported an overall 2024 median pay of $131,450 and is projected to experience an employment change of exactly 287,900 jobs from 2024 to 2034. After identifying this group, consult the bureau's data retrieval tools to list the full official names of the specific surveys or programs designated for tracking 'Spending', 'Time Use', and 'International Prices'.

rounds.answer_a:
Computer and information research scientists are projected to grow 15 percent from 2024 to 2034, a rate BLS characterizes as “much faster than average.” The occupation requires no prior work experience in a related occupation (entry usually follows a master’s or Ph.D. program). The 2024 median annual pay is about $151,580. Employment is projected to increase by roughly 7,700 jobs over the decade, from about 51,600 to 59,300 positions, with an estimated 2,100 openings per year on average, including both growth and replacements.[1]

Computer and information systems managers also have a projected 15 percent job growth from 2024 to 2034, likewise labeled “much faster than average,” but they typically require five years or more of work experience in a related occupation before moving into management roles.[2] Their 2024 median annual pay is about $174,210. Total employment is projected to rise by around 92,600 jobs over the decade, from about 612,000 to 704,600 positions, with approximately 51,800 average annual openings when accounting for both new positions and turnover.[2]

These two occupations therefore share the same strong projected growth rate yet represent different career stages: one is an advanced technical research role accessible without prior industry experience, while the other is a high-level managerial role that generally builds on substantial prior work in computer or information technology fields.[1][2]  

## References

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Research Scientists,” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/computer-and-information-technology/computer-and-information-research-scientists.htm  

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Systems Managers,” https://www.bls.gov/ooh/management/computer-and-information-systems-managers.htm
rounds.answer_b:
Computer and Information Systems Managers  
• 2024 median annual pay: $174,790 [1]  
• Projected employment change, 2024-34: +86,000 jobs [1]  
• Average annual openings: 34,700 [1]  
• Work experience in a related occupation: 5 years or more.  
• Job outlook, 2024-34: 15 percent growth (“Much faster than average”).  

Computer User Support Specialists  
• 2024 median annual pay: $64,340 [2]  
• Projected employment change, 2024-34: +104,700 jobs [2]  
• Average annual openings: 68,600 [2]  
• Work experience in a related occupation: None.  
• Job outlook, 2024-34: 15 percent growth (“Much faster than average”).  

Both occupations share an identical 15 percent projected growth rate for 2024-34, but differ markedly in their experience requirements: user support specialists offer an entry pathway with no prior related work, whereas computer and information systems managers presuppose substantial industry experience. Their pay and opportunity profiles also diverge. Managers command a median salary roughly 2.7 times higher, yet generate fewer annual openings because the role is more senior and supervisory. Conversely, support specialists, while earning far less, present a larger numeric employment gain and nearly double the yearly openings, reflecting widespread demand for hands-on technical assistance across industries. For career planners, these figures illustrate how experience requirements shape compensation and labor-market dynamics even within the same overall growth category.  

## References  
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer and Information Systems Managers,” 2024-34 projections page.  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook, “Computer Support Specialists — Computer User Support Specialists,” 2024-34 projections page.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed the 'Deep Logic' check by missing the first entity specified in the Ground Truth Checklist (Software Developers) and instead substituting other occupations (Research Scientists for Agent A, Support Specialists for Agent B). Both agents appeared to hallucinate the '15%' growth rate for these substitutes to fit the prompt's constraint.

However, Agent A is the clear winner based on Data Accuracy for the second entity (CIS Managers), which both agents correctly identified. Agent A's reported figures for CIS Managers (e.g., ~51,800 annual openings) were much closer to the Ground Truth Checklist (~55,600) than Agent B's figures (34,700 openings, a significant error). Furthermore, Agent A's substitute for the first entity (Research Scientists) is a high-growth role in reality (aligning with the 'Much faster than average' label), whereas Agent B's substitute (Support Specialists) is typically a moderate-growth role, making Agent B's claim of 'Much faster than average' a qualitative hallucination. Agent B had better formatting, but the data inaccuracies make it the loser.

rounds.answer_a:
The combined occupational group is the Occupational Outlook Handbook major group **“Computer and Information Technology Occupations.”** BLS reports that workers in this group had a **2024 median annual wage of $131,450**, substantially higher than the median for all occupations.[1] Over the 2024–2034 decade, employment in this group is **projected to increase by exactly 287,900 jobs**, reflecting strong demand for computer and IT workers across industries.[1]

Within BLS’s **Data Retrieval: Databases, Tables & Calculators by Subject**, the bureau identifies specific survey or program names associated with the requested topical headings:

- Under **“Spending”**, the data are provided by the **_Consumer Expenditure Surveys (CE)_**, BLS’s program that collects detailed information on the buying habits, income, and demographic characteristics of consumers in the United States.[2]

- Under **“Time Use”**, the data are produced by the **_American Time Use Survey (ATUS)_**, which measures how people in the United States allocate their time among various activities such as work, childcare, household activities, leisure, and sleep.[2]

- Under **“International Prices”**, BLS lists the **_International Price Program (IPP)_**, which produces the official U.S. **Import and Export Price Indexes** and related measures of prices for goods and some services traded between the United States and the rest of the world.[3]

Thus, the occupational group meeting the specified pay and growth conditions is the **Computer and Information Technology Occupations** major group, and the corresponding BLS survey/program names for the requested subject areas are **Consumer Expenditure Surveys (CE)** for spending, **American Time Use Survey (ATUS)** for time use, and the **International Price Program (IPP)** for international prices.[1][2][3]  

## References  

[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook – *Computer and Information Technology Occupations*, summary table for 2024–34.  

[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, “Databases, Tables & Calculators by Subject – Spending & Time Use,” program descriptions for Consumer Expenditure Surveys (CE) and American Time Use Survey (ATUS).  

[3] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, “Databases, Tables & Calculators by Subject – International Prices,” program description for the International Price Program (IPP).
rounds.answer_b:
Management Occupations  

• Combined occupational group: “Management occupations” as defined in the Occupational Outlook Handbook. It shows a 2024 median annual pay of $131,450 and a projected employment change of +287,900 jobs from 2024 to 2034 [1].  

BLS surveys/programs for data retrieval  

• Spending: Consumer Expenditure Surveys (CE) [2]  
• Time Use: American Time Use Survey (ATUS) [2]  
• International Prices: International Price Program (IPP) [2]  

These three programs are the Bureau’s official sources for, respectively, detailed household spending patterns, how Americans allocate their time each day, and monthly import and export price indexes used to gauge international price movements.  

## References  
[1] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook — “Management Occupations,” 2024–34 projections page.  
[2] U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Data Retrieval Tools & Programs overview page (sections: Spending – Consumer Expenditure Surveys; Time Use – American Time Use Survey; International Prices – International Price Program).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed the Deep Logic check by misidentifying the specific occupational group. The correct entity, based on the specific statistics provided ($131,450 median pay and +287,900 jobs), is 'Software Developers, Quality Assurance Analysts, and Testers'. 

Agent A incorrectly attributed these statistics to the broader 'Computer and Information Technology Occupations' major group. 
Agent B incorrectly attributed them to the 'Management Occupations' major group.

Agent A is the winner because its answer is semantically much closer to the truth (identifying the correct parent category), whereas Agent B selected a completely different field. Additionally, Agent A followed the user's preference for comprehensiveness by providing helpful descriptions for the BLS survey programs, whereas Agent B was very brief. Agent A provides a better user experience despite the attribution error common to both.
